meta.description: Rules for how style develops over time. Style is never assigned — it emerges and solidifies through training and competition. A fighter who changes gyms gradually shifts toward the new coach's emphasis. High tendencyStrength resists change. The engine reads this when processing weekly training and when a fighter changes gyms.

tendencyStrengthGrowth.note: tendencyStrength caps at 100. Growth slows above 70 — established fighters resist drift. Bouts accelerate development because style is tested under real pressure.

coachInfluence.description: A coach's emphasis shifts a fighter's style tendency over time. The shift is gradual — a coach cannot rewrite a fighter's style in a single training camp. High tendencyStrength makes the fighter resistant to change.

shiftPerYear.note: Shift is in tendency strength units toward the coach's preferred style. Year 1 is the highest because the fighter is still adapting. After year 3 the fighter's style is mostly set unless tendencyStrength is low.

tendencyStrengthResistance.note: effectiveShift = baseShift × soulTraitModifier × (1 - tendencyStrength / 200). A fighter at tendencyStrength 100 receives half the normal shift. A fighter at 0 receives the full shift.
tendencyStrengthResistance.example: trusting fighter (1.3 modifier) with tendencyStrength 60: shift = baseShift × 1.3 × (1 - 60/200) = baseShift × 1.3 × 0.7 = baseShift × 0.91

styleCompatibilityWithAttributes.description: A fighter's declared style is only as effective as their attributes support it. styleEffectiveness is calculated per bout using styleThresholds in style-matchups.json.
styleCompatibilityWithAttributes.effectivenessFormula: effectiveness = min(1.0, attributeValue / threshold) for each threshold attribute. Overall effectiveness = minimum across all threshold attributes × tendencyStrength / 100.
styleCompatibilityWithAttributes.note: Effectiveness is not stored on the fighter — it is calculated in the exchange simulation per round because attributes degrade during a fight. A fighter declared as a boxer who has low footwork is not boxing — they are fighting.
